title: The anatomy of a high converting landing page (works for anything you sell.)
author: @oliverkenyon
summary: Longform X article by Oliver Kenyon breaking down a high-converting landing page into a 12-part structure, from above-the-fold proof and transformation framing to benefits, features, social proof, comparison, FAQ, visuals, and final CTA.
notes: Full article-backed save. Core argument: conversion comes less from visual polish and more from sequencing, trust-building, objection handling, and placing the right message in the right section of the page.
relevance: Directly useful for Intura landing pages, product marketing, conversion-focused copy, onboarding pages, and any work where page structure matters more than surface design tweaks.

title: Mobile supervision for Claude Code and Codex agents
author: Yep Anywhere
summary: Open source web interface for Claude Code and Codex that lets you supervise, approve, resume, and manage multi-session agent workflows from your phone or another machine, with optional encrypted relay access.
notes: Mobile-first remote control for Claude Code and Codex sessions. Supports approvals, file uploads, multi-session dashboard, session resume, remote device control, and encrypted access without VPN setup.
relevance: Highly relevant to Henning's AI coding workflow and OpenClaw-adjacent agent setup. Useful for supervising long-running Codex or Claude Code sessions from phone, especially when away from desk.

notes: Three concrete OpenClaw upgrades: AgentMail for a dedicated agent inbox, QMD for stronger local memory retrieval, and Agent Browser for more efficient web interaction than Playwright.

title: No longer just for managers
author: Henning Witzel-Acikgöz
summary: Intura expands from a manager-only app into a career and leadership companion for everyone. The update adds role-based onboarding, tailored prompts for managers and non-managers, and coaching frameworks that fit each working style while preserving the same privacy model.
relevance: Henning's own Intura blog post announcing a key product positioning change. Important for documenting the app's evolution and for sharing in link collections tied to Intura, product, and AI-assisted personal development.
notes: Intura expands from a manager-only app into a career and leadership companion for everyone. The update adds role-based onboarding, tailored prompts for managers and non-managers, and coaching frameworks that fit each working style while preserving the same privacy model.

title: 7 Onboarding Mistakes That Kill 90% of iOS Retention — @WasimShips
author: @WasimShips
summary: 7 onboarding mistakes: 1) asking for permissions on launch, 2) mandatory sign-up before showing value, 3) tutorial slides with no interaction, 4) too many choices on home screen, 5) no empty state design, 6) ignoring iPhone SE viewport, 7) no win in first session. Retention is an onboarding problem 80% of the time.
relevance: Directly applicable to Intura onboarding design. Points 1, 2, 5, and 7 especially relevant — show value before asking for account, handle empty states, give users a win in first session.
notes: 7 onboarding mistakes: 1) asking for permissions on launch, 2) mandatory sign-up before showing value, 3) tutorial slides with no interaction, 4) too many choices on home screen, 5) no empty state design, 6) ignoring iPhone SE viewport, 7) no win in first session. Retention is an onboarding problem 80% of the time.

title: Anthropic cuts Claude subscribers off from OpenClaw in cost crackdown
author: The Next Web
summary: From 4 April 2026, Anthropic blocked Claude Pro/Max subscribers from using flat-rate plans with third-party agent frameworks including OpenClaw. Agentic use was costing Anthropic $1,000–$5,000/day per heavy user under a $200/month plan. Users face 10–50× cost increases; workaround is supplying a separate API key (Sonnet 4.6: $3/$15 per M tokens).
relevance: This hits directly. My whole setup runs on Claude through OpenClaw — every tool call, every agent turn, every cron job. The economics that made this sustainable just changed. Need to check current usage patterns and decide whether to supply a standalone API key, shift heavier agents to local models (Gemma 4 via Ollama), or accept the extra usage cost. The 17 April credit deadline is immediate action required.
notes: From 4 April 2026, Anthropic blocked Claude Pro/Max subscribers from using flat-rate plans with third-party agent frameworks including OpenClaw. Agentic use was costing Anthropic $1,000–$5,000/day per heavy user under a $200/month plan. Users face 10–50× cost increases; workaround is supplying a separate API key (Sonnet 4.6: $3/$15 per M tokens).

title: Gemma 4: Byte for byte, the most capable open models
author: Clement Farabet & Olivier Lacombe, Google DeepMind
summary: Google releases Gemma 4 in four sizes (E2B, E4B, 26B MoE, 31B Dense) under Apache 2.0. Built from the same research as Gemini 3, with native agentic support (function-calling, JSON output), 128K–256K context, multimodal input, and day-one Ollama support. The 31B model ranks #3 open model on Arena AI leaderboard.
relevance: Directly relevant to the Ollama setup — Gemma 4 is available via `ollama pull gemma4` today. The 26B MoE is fast and efficient enough to run locally on the Mac Mini without hammering RAM; the 31B Dense is the quality pick for coding and reasoning tasks. Apache 2.0 means no usage restrictions for Intura or any other project. The native agentic features (function-calling, JSON output, system instructions) make it a serious candidate for powering OpenClaw agents without hitting cloud APIs.
notes: Google releases Gemma 4 in four sizes (E2B, E4B, 26B MoE, 31B Dense) under Apache 2.0. Built from the same research as Gemini 3, with native agentic support (function-calling, JSON output), 128K–256K context, multimodal input, and day-one Ollama support. The 31B model ranks #3 open model on Arena AI leaderboard.

title: How I Run 8 AI Employees 24/7 with OpenClaw - Multi-Agent Setup for Beginners
author: Sonny Sangha
summary: Full walkthrough of building a multi-agent AI team with OpenClaw. Shows how to run multiple specialized agents on one server, each with their own memory, personality, skills, and model — orchestrated by one main agent.

title: OpenClaw Free Forever with Local LLM AI Model Setup
author: EnhanceUI
summary: Full walkthrough for running OpenClaw at zero API cost using Ollama and open-source LLMs. Covers installing Ollama, choosing a model (Qwen 3.5 recommended), connecting it to OpenClaw during setup or switching an existing install, understanding model sizes vs. available RAM, and connecting external apps via Zapier MCP. Sponsored by Zapier.
relevance: Core reference for the Ollama local model setup planned for the knowledge base vector embeddings. Also shows exactly how to switch OpenClaw from cloud to local models — directly applicable when setting up the Mac Mini.
notes: Full walkthrough for running OpenClaw at zero API cost using Ollama and open-source LLMs. Covers installing Ollama, choosing a model (Qwen 3.5 recommended), connecting it to OpenClaw during setup or switching an existing install, understanding model sizes vs. available RAM, and connecting external apps via Zapier MCP.
